author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

originalBook: In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.
title: Chapter 106

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Chapter 106.—240.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "Woe unto you, therefore, who, by doing violence to what is holy, cut away the bond of unity; whereas the prophet says, ‘If the people shall sin, the priest shall pray for them: but if the priest shall sin, who will pray for him?’"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: I seemed too a little while ago, when we were disputing about the oil of the sinner, to anoint your forehead, in order that you might say, if you dared, whether you yourself were not a sinner.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You have had the hardihood to say as much.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What a portentous sin!

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For in that you assert yourself to be a priest, what else have you maintained by quoting this testimony of the prophet, save that you are wholly without sin?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if you have sin, who is there that shall pray for you, according to your understanding of the words?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For thus you blazon yourselves among the wretched people, quoting from the prophet: "If the people shall sin, the priest shall pray for them: but if the priest shall sin, who will pray for him? to the intent that they may believe you to be without sin, and entrust the wiping away their sins to your prayers.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Truly ye are great men, exalted above your fellows, heavenly, godlike, angels indeed rather than men, who pray for the people, and will not have the people pray for you!

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Are you more righteous than Paul, more perfect than that great apostle, who was wont to commend himself to the prayers of those whom he taught?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: "Continue," he says, "in prayer, and watch in the same with thanksgiving; withal praying also for us, that God would open unto us a door of utterance, to speak the mystery of Christ, for which I am also in bonds; that I may make it manifest, as I ought to speak."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: See how prayer is made for an apostle, which you would have not made for a bishop.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Do you perceive of how devilish a nature your pride is?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Prayer is made for an apostle, that he may make manifest the mystery of Christ as he ought to speak.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Accordingly, if you had a pious people under you, you ought to have exhorted them to pray for you, that you might not give utterance as you ought not.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Are you more righteous than the evangelist John, who says, "If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Finally, are you more righteous than Daniel, whom you yourself quoted in this very epistle, going so far as to say, "The most righteous king cast forth Daniel, as he supposed, to be devoured by wild beasts?"—a thing which he never did suppose, since he said to Daniel himself, in the most friendly spirit, as the context of the lesson shows, "Thy God, whom thou servest continually, He will deliver thee."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But on this subject we have already said much.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: With regard to the question now before us, viz., that Daniel was most righteous, it is proved not by your testimony, though that might be sufficient for me in the argument which I hold with you, but by the testimony of the Spirit of God, speaking also by the mouth of Ezekiel, where he named three men of most eminent righteousness, Noah, Daniel, and Job, who, he said, were the only men that could be saved from a certain excessive wrath of God, which was hanging over all the rest.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: A man, therefore, of the highest righteousness, one of three conspicuous for righteousness, prays, and says, "While I was speaking, and praying, and confessing my sin, and the sin of my people Israel, and presenting my supplication before the Lord my God."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And you say that you are without sin, because forsooth you are a priest; and if the people sin, you pray for them: but if you sin, who shall pray for you?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For clearly by the impiety of such arrogance you show yourself to be unworthy of the mediation of that Priest whom the prophet would have to be understood in these words, which you do not understand.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For now that no one may ask why this was said, I will explain it so far as by God’s grace I shall be able.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: God was preparing the minds of men, by His prophet, to desire a Priest of such a sort that none should pray for Him.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: He was Himself prefigured in the times of the first people and the first temple, in which all things were figures for our ensample.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Therefore the high priest used to enter alone into the holy of holies, that he might make supplication for the people, which did not enter with the priest into that inner sanctuary; just as our High Priest is entered into the secret places of the heavens, into that truer holy of holies, whilst we for whom He prays are still placed here.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: It is with this reference that the prophet says, "If the people shall sin, the priest shall pray for them: but if the priest shall sin, who will pray for him?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Seek therefore a priest of such a kind that he cannot sin, nor need that one should pray for him.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And for this reason prayer is made for the apostles by the people; but for that Priest who is the Master and Lord of the apostles is prayer not made.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Hear John confessing this, and saying, "My little children, these things write I unto you, that ye sin not.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And if any man sin, we have an Advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous, and He is the propitiation for our sins."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: "We have," he says; and "for our sins."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: I pray you, learn humility, that you may not fall, or rather, that in time you may arise again.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For had you not already fallen, you never would have used such words.
